column2: Personally Identifiable Information; any data that can identify an individual.
Protected Health Information; sensitive medical data linked to an individual (HIPAA).
General Data Protection Regulation; comprehensive EU law for data protection and privacy.
The entity that determines the "purposes and means" of data processing.
An entity that processes data on behalf of a Data Controller.
Framework ensuring privacy is built into systems from the initial design phase.
Principle of collecting only the minimum amount of data required for a specific purpose.
Principle stating data should only be used for the reason it was originally collected.
Principle stating data should be kept no longer than necessary for its purpose.
The irreversible process of removing identifiers from a dataset.
Replacing identifiers with aliases; reversible using a separate key or mapping.
Replacing sensitive data (like credit card numbers) with a non-sensitive surrogate (token).
Obfuscating sensitive data fields with realistic but fake values for testing/dev.
A technique that adds statistical noise to a dataset to protect individual records.
Privacy property where each record is indistinguishable from at least k-1 others.
An extension of k-anonymity that ensures sensitive attributes have sufficient variety.
Combining non-sensitive data points to reveal more sensitive information.
Deducing sensitive information by analyzing patterns in non-sensitive data.
The concept that data is subject to the laws of the country where it is physically located.
Data Subject Access Request; an individual's request to see what data a company holds on them.
The right of a data subject to request the deletion of their personal data.
Foundational principles for fair information practices and data protection.
US standard for protecting electronic PHI (ePHI).
US law requiring financial institutions to safeguard customer information.
US law governing the online collection of data from children under 13.
